author: Serhii Voloshyn
msg:
Here's why this problem arose and why static IP solved it:
1. The problem of "collective responsibility" (CGNAT)
Your previous address (100.64.107.114) was an internal address of the provider. This means that hundreds or even thousands of subscribers were accessing the Internet through the same common public IP address.
What happened: If someone else from your city (who is on the same provider) was doing suspicious things, sending spam, or simply updating the application too often, the server's security systems (Firewall/WAF) put this common IP address on the "blacklist".
Result: When you tried to connect, the server saw a "banned" address and gave you a 403 Forbidden, although you personally did not violate anything.
2. Specifics of the WebSocket protocol
Applications often use WebSocket for instant messages or updates. Unlike regular websites, WebSocket requires a long and stable connection.
Provider gateways (NAT) that serve dynamic addresses often "aggressively" terminate old connections to make room for new users.
The application server could view your router's attempt to break through this "double barrier" as an attack or a malformed request.

3. Why did it work before and then stop?
This could happen for three reasons:
Address pool change: The provider could have changed the routing, and you ended up in a subnet that was already in the spam filter database.
Application security update: Developers could have tightened security rules by prohibiting connections from addresses belonging to large provider networks (this is often done to combat bots).
Provider neighbor: Someone from your network segment began to show activity that activated server protection.

author: Joshua F
msg:
Thanks Calus, at the moment it’s hard for me to tell whether Unity is a fully dedicated standalone effort, or something being run alongside other MN/WM priorities with staff split across multiple workstreams.

As a UNO, that leaves me unclear on where Unity is heading and when, who owns what, and how we should plan or support over the next 6–12 months. It also feels like a number of essential roles and product features are still missing, without a clear plan or timeline for when they’ll land.

I’m optimistic about what Unity can achieve, but feel like some transparency here from the team would really help to tie everything together and allow us to properly prepare for whats ahead.

author: ⚡️BeyondLimitsNodes⚡️
msg: How close are we to some other payout options?

author: Calus B
msg:
So it’s a joint venture between world, Mobile, and and network as you probably know. It is split I believe 50-50 between the two companies. It exists to serve customers that minutes network and world mobile individually cannot serve. As such you will see both World mobile and minutes  network teams. Interacting. 
Specifically Unity will have its own sales and onboarding teams for its clients. Josh overseas much of the technical details as well as other members of the minutes teams (Omri  Dork ect) Jamie is working hard on many of the partnerships in web 3. They do push a lot of the grunt work out to third-party vendors as well. Josh has mentioned this previously. 

Unity is at its core supposed to be a for-profit business that pays the node operators to manage licenses and then provisions tasks for those license operators to do. They are sort of the facilitators making high-quality testing environments for telecom companies. Obviously on the network side, they’re using the sharing economy, but on the telecom side, they are working more as a traditional business. It’s sort of a hybrid. 

To somewhat address, your concerns about where we are going and how fast we are going there at least from an ambassador level I will say this. Unity is only possible because of the maturation of world mobile and minutes network. It is an absolutely necessary part of their business model. I would expect the highest levels of attention to be paid on it. I know at this point they haven’t shared many details about their plans. We’ve seen scout and runner we’ve seen some CLI tasks and some basic telemetry tasks. They’ve hinted at many more tasks that will be rolled out. I think these are the tasks that you will see over the next 6 to 12 months. Beyond that as the network matures, they have some broader plans to help support license operators specifically those who are doing manual tasks as well with additional ability to earn ups. The beauty of what they’re building is it can be utilized in so many broad applications once it’s going. It’s going to be one of the Crown Jewels of this ecosystem they are building.

author: RS
msg: There’s a few folks like yourself who could join the team in communications such roles. I think the team is very lean, so I do understand Joshua’s questions. I also want to know how many engineers/developers, sales, marketing, ops etc (how many total people does Unity employ?) - if UNOs are expected to make Mini subsidiary companies than we need to know this. I do like production direction thus far.

author: R B
msg: has anyone had a CLI call yet

author: Calus B
msg: I think of you UNO’s sort of as franchisees. Now you can just exist, and the brand of Unity will sort of carry you along. Meaning just by being involved with the brand you will make probably pretty decent money. However, if you were interested in maximizing your revenue, there’s lots of things you could actively do to make sure that you earn a great deal more than sort of the baseline.

author: Joshua F
msg:
If UNOs are effectively Unity’s franchisees, then they’re not setting us up to succeed yet. We’re expected to market and do licensee management but there’s still very limited guidance, tools and training, unclear comms, and little forward visibility so we’re left speculating.

It seems like this speculation  is increasingly fuelling negative sentiment, which can spill online and kneecap the project early. Don’t want to be pessimistic but this verified group feels more like sentiment contagion than benefit to us UNOs. I’ve got strong confidence in the team delivering the technical telecoms side, I just think the franchise/community layer needs the same level of attention, with clearer expectations and enablement so we can do the job we’ve paid to do.

author: Calus B
msg: There’s a lot of stuff coming.  It’s very much a chicken in the egg scenario where you needed to get the network going in some form or another to start to roll these jobs out.. my earlier post was regarding the longer-term vision. They are absolutely aware of the needed support and will absolutely get those tools in our hands.

msg: It’s just gonna take some time and there are things they’re gonna prioritize before that like earnings and new task families

author: Joshua F
msg: My reason for asking about the team is that from the outside it all feels operationally messy. Timelines have shifted so many times. Things get introduced then removed. We were told in october that we’d be getting double Unity rewards for 6 months and that round 1 was about to close - how was that ever going to work? How did it get all the way to announcement before being scrapped? I hear you Calus but the team are showing a pattern with the appearance of winging it alongside other priorities. It would not be difficult for them to prove otherwise, but they havn’t. A basic forward plan would go a long way.

author: Calus B
msg: Yeah, it does feel operationally messy. I think in part it’s because of how many moving pieces there are and in part for sure things falling through the cracks in terms of getting re-prioritized and as always, the timelines stretching. I’m honestly not sure due to the difficulty of what they’re trying to do in the novelty of it all if we could expect much different. Perhaps the one expectation would be if we could have a more clear community liaison that handles most of the lower level communication. That could perhaps move some of the rough areas that have.

author: Quoia75
msg:
**🌈Dream scenario – A Node Management Website that would allow UNOs to:**

- Easily move batches of licenses between nodes. In that way nodes could be treated like folders for managing node-specific splits for reward tiers or groups of ULOs.

- Assign a custom name to each node.

- Assign a unique QR Code to each node that when scanned by a new ULO would automatically generate a lease code that’s forwarded to the ULO via email or SMS.

- Create and assign a ticker to the front of licenses so they are easy to find by a UNOs own ULOs browsing the marketplace.

- Track earnings by license / by node on an Analytics UI

- A License/node health monitor UI

- Allow reports to be generated and downloaded as CSV files or PDFs

- Allow the bulk generation of lease codes


….what am I missing?

author: Peterjo18
msg:
I would rather see referrer groups.

Each referrer group can have its own unique split 

Single link for a referrer group to pick up a license from your pool of available licenses

author: Quoia75
msg:
Good one.👍

I think because the approach to marketing and license deployment is going to be so varied given each node owner's goals, the release of a public API will be the ultimate solution, while some basic prized features like bulk license management should be baked in.
